Sodium and Potassium Chloride Refluxes 31
Equilibrium Concentration of Lithium Amalgam for Close Cycle Reflux at $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ 97

By the first part of 1953, the program for the separation of lithium isotopes by chemical methods had expanded and matured to such a point that it was believed an interim report condensing the chemistry under one cover would be advantageous.

It may be seen in Figure 1 that the heart of the process is an isotopic exchange and enrichment section which in most instances is a column in which lithium amalgam flows countercurrent to a lithium salt dissolved in a suitable solvent.
The product in the separation of lithium isotopes is enriched in lithium six (Li $^6$ ) and the waste is enriched in lithium seven (Li $^7$ ).
A simplified sketch of a dual temperature system for the separation of lithium isotopes is presented in Figure 2.
The theory of the dual temperature process is not given in this report but may be found in the work of Spivack (114), Eidinoff (115), and Demarcus (116).
Separation of lithium isotopes in a system of lithium amalgam in contact with lithium chloride dissolved in ethyl alcohol was described by Lewis and MacDonald (110) in 1936.

This important contribution indicated that isotopic exchange took place with astonishing rapidity, that the separation factor $(\alpha)$ per stage was at least 1.025, and that the heavier isotope was found preferentially in the alcohol phase indicating that chemical bonding of lithium in the solvent was stronger than it was in the amalgam.
\alpha = \frac {\left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {6} / \mathrm {L i} ^ {7}\right)}{\left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {6} / \mathrm {L i} ^ {7}\right)} - \text {a m a l g a m}
In this latter class of solvents, propylenediamine (1,2, diamine propane) has received the most attention.
One method of representing the chemical separation of lithium isotopes is illustrated in Figure 3.
\mathrm {L i} ^ {7} (\text {a m a l g a m}) \rightarrow \left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {7}\right) ^ {+} (\mathrm {P D A}) + \mathrm {e}, \Delta \mathrm {F} ^ {\mathrm {O}} = - \mathrm {A} \tag {1}
\mathrm {L i} ^ {6} (\text {a m a l g a m}) \rightarrow \left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {6}\right) ^ {+} (\mathrm {P D A}) + e, \Delta F ^ {0} = - B \tag {2}
Subtracting equation (2) from (1),

\mathrm {L i} ^ {7} (\text {a m a l g a m}) + \left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {6}\right) ^ {+} (\mathrm {P D A}) \rightarrow \mathrm {L i} ^ {6} (\text {a m a l g a m}) + \left(\mathrm {L i} ^ {7}\right) ^ {+} (\mathrm {P D A}) \tag {3}
\Delta \mathbf {F} ^ {\mathrm {O}} = - \mathbf {A} + \mathbf {B} = - \mathbf {C} \tag {4}
Since our illustration is greatly exaggerated, $-C$ does not represent a very large free energy change ( $\Delta F^0 \cong -31$ cal. at $25^\circ C$ for the system in question).
where $\Delta H^0$ and $\Delta S^0$ are assumed to be independent of temperature over small temperature changes.
The binomial approximation that $\alpha - 1$ is equal to $\log_{e} \alpha$ contains an error of less than $3\%$ up to $\alpha = 1.06$ .

The assumption that $\Delta H^0$ and $\Delta S^0$ are independent of temperature over the range of temperature in which isotopic separation factors have been measured (0 - $100^{0}\mathrm{C}$ ) appears to be as accurate as the analytical methods available for the determination of isotopic abundance.
Other ideas concerning the dependence of separation factors on temperature have been given by Begun, Drury and Palko (111).

The application of Gibbs adsorption thermodynamics to data obtained by Convers (26) and by Johnston and Ubbelohde (112) indicates adsorption (surface excess) of alkali metals at amalgam surfaces.

If the reaction is diffusion controlled, the following factors appear to be important to the amalgam phase or solvent phase which ever is rate controlling: (1) agitation, (2) concentration, (3) viscosity, and (4) temperature.
It may be seen from Figure 4 that the separation factor in several lithium systems is a function of temperature.
The operability of the dual temperature process has been demonstrated by the Materials Chemistry Division (See ORNL reports 1238, 1306, 1401, YB 35-12, and ORNL CF 53-4-299).

Propylenediamine appears to have the widest useful temperature range of from room temperature to $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; however, the stability of propylenediamine above $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the presence of an alkali amalgam has not been well established.
A schematic representation of the dual temperature process is shown in Figure 2.
2 \mathrm {L i O H} _ {\mathrm {a q}} + (\text {p o t e n t i a l}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) + 1 / 2 \mathrm {O} _ {2} \text {g a s} + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} \tag {7}
\mathrm {L i C l} _ {\mathrm {a q}} + (\text {p o t e n t i a l}) \rightarrow \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) + 1 / 2 \mathrm {C l} _ {2} \text {g a s} \tag {8}
\mathrm {L i C l} _ {\text {o r g}} + \mathrm {N a} (\mathrm {H g}) + (\text {p o t e n t i a l}) \rightarrow \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) + \mathrm {N a C l} _ {(\text {i n s o l u b l e})} (9)
\operatorname {L i C l} (\text {f u s e d s a l t w i t h K C l}) + (\text {p o t e n t i a l}) \rightarrow \operatorname {L i} (\text {m e t a l}) + 1 / 2 \operatorname {C l} _ {2} (1 0)
Industrial equipment and methods are available for the processes utilizing reactions (7), (8), and (10).
Current efficiencies of $90\%$ or higher have been reported for such installations.
Reaction (9) which has a mercury cathode and a sodium amalgam anode would require an extensive cell development program for successful application as a plant reflux method.
Reactions (8) and (10) if applied to organic systems require the desolvation of the lithium chloride.
\left(\mathrm {E}\right) + 2 \mathrm {L i O H} + (\mathrm {H g}) \xrightarrow {\mathrm {k} ^ {\prime}} 2 \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + 1 / 2 \mathrm {O} _ {2}
d \frac {[ L i (H g) ]}{d t} = k ^ {\prime} f [ (E), L i O H, L i (H g), O _ {2}, T ] \tag {11}
d \frac {[ L i (H g) ]}{d t} = k ^ {\prime} f (E) \tag {12}
\mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} \xrightarrow {\mathrm {k} _ {2}} [ \mathrm {L i O H} ] \mathrm {a q} + 1 / 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} (\text {g a s}) + \mathrm {H g}
- d \frac {\left[ L i (H g) \right]}{d t} = k _ {2} f \left\{\left[ L i (H g) \right], L i O H, S, T \right\} \tag {13}
These variables are known to effect the rate 29,30.
- d \frac {\left[ L i (H g) \right]}{d t} = k _ {2} ^ {\prime} \left[ L i (H g) \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {14}
\mathbf {k} ^ {\prime} = \frac {\left[ \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) \right] _ {\mathrm {o}} ^ {1 / 2} - \left[ \mathrm {L i} (\mathrm {H g}) \right] _ {\mathrm {f}} ^ {1 / 2}}{\mathrm {t}} \tag {15}
d \frac {\left[ L i (H g) \right]}{d t} = k _ {1} ^ {\prime} f (E) - k _ {2} ^ {\prime} \left[ L i (H g) \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {16}
Equation 16 qualitatively defines the kinetics of the "Elex" process.

For example, if $\mathbf{k}_1' \cdot f(\mathbf{E})$ is greater than $\mathbf{k}_2' \cdot [\mathrm{Li}(\mathrm{Hg})]^{1/2}$ , the reaction rate is positive and lithium is transferred from the aqueous to the amalgam phase.

Finally, when $\mathbf{k}_1' \cdot f(\mathbf{E})$ is less than $\mathbf{k}_2' \cdot [\mathrm{Li}(\mathrm{Hg})]^{1/2}$ the rate is negative and net decomposition of the amalgam occurs to provide a product end reflux.
Similar treatment could be extended to the reactions represented by equations 8-10; however, little is to be gained by such a generalized development.
M g a m a l g a m + 2 L i C l (P D A s o l ^ {\prime} n) \rightarrow L i a m a l g a m + M g C l _ {2} \cdot 3 P D A \tag {17}
Reaction (17) proceeds very near completion because of the high stability and insolubility of the magnesium chloride solvate with propylenediamine.
However, the equilibrium ratio of lithium to magnesium in the amalgam as a function of temperature or amine concentration has not been fully investigated (57).

Laboratory batch data (90, 91, 92 and 100) indicate that the conversion of magnesium amalgam to lithium amalgam follows a first order rate with respect to magnesium in the amalgam and to be independent of lithium chloride concentration in the amine phase as long as it was in stoichiometric excess.
- d \frac {\left[ M g (H g) \right]}{d t} = k [ M g (H g) ] \tag {18}
\ln \frac {\left[ \mathrm {M g} (\mathrm {H g}) \right] _ {\mathrm {o}}}{\left[ \mathrm {M g} (\mathrm {H g}) \right] _ {\mathrm {f}}} = \mathrm {k t} \tag {19}
\mathrm {M g} \text {a m a l g a m} + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {2} \mathrm {O} \rightarrow \mathrm {M g} (\mathrm {O H}) _ {2} + \mathrm {H} _ {2} \tag {20}
Although propylenediamine is a very hydroscopic solvent, reaction (20) is likely to contribute heavily to preventing any buildup of water in the solvent.

Chemical methods for recovery of propylenediamine by cleavage of the $\mathrm{MgCl}_2\cdot 3\mathrm{PDA}$ structure fall into two classes: (1) replacement of the propylenediamine with a cheaper chemical having the ability of forming a more stable solvate and (2) chemical reaction to form new salts that do not have stable solvates with propylenediamine.
\mathrm {M g C l} _ {2} \cdot 3 \mathrm {P D A} + 6 \mathrm {N H} _ {3} \rightarrow \mathrm {M g C l} _ {2} \cdot 6 \mathrm {N H} _ {3} + 3 \mathrm {P D A} \tag {21}
The same reaction has been studied using methanol as a solvent for the solvate, $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 3\mathsf{PDA}$ .
In this instance, the amount of excess propylenediamine accompanying the solvate, $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 3\mathsf{PDA}$ , limits the solubility of the solvate.
Apparently, lithium chloride dissolved in the accompanying propylenediamine also limits the solvate, $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 3\mathsf{PDA}$ , solubility (see Figure 55).

The degree of completion of the precipitation of $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 6\mathsf{NH}_3$ from a methanol solution, $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 3\mathsf{PDA}$ is highly dependent upon the concentration of ammonia.
Some propylenediamine has been found in the precipitate, $\mathsf{MgCl}_2\cdot 6\mathsf{NH}_3$ , as may be seen in Figure 57.
2 \mathrm {N a O H} + \mathrm {M g C l} _ {2} \cdot 3 \mathrm {P D A} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M g} (\mathrm {O H}) _ {2} + 2 \mathrm {N a C l} + 3 \mathrm {P D A} \tag {22}
Regardless of the solvent used for reaction (22) the rate of reaction and equilibrium are very favorable for the release of propylenediamine.

text: If the general behavior predicted by the equations given in Table 3 holds then from equilibrium considerations a sodium chloride system should yield a better reflux than bromide or iodide in that a lower concentration of lithium remaining the amalgam per given stage would result.

The tolerance for water in propylenediamine is believed to be at least as low as methods of analysis (less than $0.01\%$ by weight) for propylenediamine systems utilizing a waste end reflux with magnesium, potassium, or sodium amalgam.
In order to obtain anhydrous lithium chloride drying at temperatures above $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is required.
Fortunately, little if any decomposition to form hydrogen chloride takes place in the drying operation (55).
